text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in the right hand to separate the outer ovarian cortex from the inner ovarian cyst, while holding a traumatic grasper in the left hand. The dense and tense endometrioma is manipulated, and the ovarian cyst is carefully dissected, with potential rupture requiring suction and lavage.

The ruptured ovarian cyst releases thick chocolate-colored material. A 5mm suction device is introduced to aspirate the contents, with additional irrigation to dilute the material for easier removal. Occasionally, a 10mm suction is used. The surgical team performs copious lavage to clean the cyst, ensuring the patient's head is elevated to prevent fluid from moving to the upper abdomen.

The surgeon performs repeated suction and irrigation using at least three liters of saline to clear the surgical field. The cystectomy is underway, with the surgeon using a traumatic grasper to hold the inner cystic layer, facilitating the stripping of the thick and densely adhered endometrial tissue. Minor bleeding occurs during the cystectomy, but the surgeon avoids immediate coagulation, carefully separating the cyst along a defined plane.

The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper in the left hand to firmly hold the ovarian cyst, facilitating its removal. The cyst is gradually stripped and removed, leaving only a small attachment. The uterine manipulator is used for anteversion, revealing the right ovary adhered to the posterior uterine wall due to endometriosis, which requires separation to prevent potential rectal involvement.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate endometriotic adhesions from the posterior wall of the uterus, avoiding the use of monopolar cautery near the uterosacral ligament. Following dissection, the surgeon uses a monopolar spatula in spray mode for fulguration, applying coagulation current from a distance to minimize the chance of recurrence by targeting remaining endometrial tissue.

The surgeon uses a spark wave technique to fulgurate the superficial endometriotic spots from a distance, avoiding direct contact to prevent deeper tissue injury. Following fulguration, suction irrigation is performed multiple times to cleanse the area. An adhesive barrier, Interceed, is applied to prevent adhesion formation.

The surgeon spreads the interseed, a dehydrated regenerated cellulose adhesive barrier, across the entire cul-de-sac to cover the endometriotic area. The ovary is repositioned posteriorly to ensure the interseed is placed between the ovary and the cul-de-sac, preventing adhesion formation.

The surgeon spreads the interseed across the cul-de-sac, ensuring it covers the endometriotic area. Saline is applied, and excess is suctioned without disturbing the interseed. The uterus is dropped, and the uterine manipulator is removed. An endobag, made from gloves, is used to contain tissue, with its edges held by a semi-traumatic grasper.

The surgeon places the mouth of the endobag into the cannula and slowly pulls the cannula out. The port closure is performed using the varus needle technique, creating a loop within a loop to pull the suture out, approximating the muscle and rectus tissue.
